1: peri de hōn egrapsate (now about the matters you wrote about) — the first of seven such formula-openings in 1 Corinthians (7:1; 7:25; 8:1; 12:1; 16:1; 16:12), signaling Paul's shift to answering the Corinthians' own questions. kalon anthrōpō gynaikos mē haptesthai (it is good for a man not to touch a woman) — another Corinthian slogan Paul quotes. Haptomai (touch) is a standard euphemism for sexual intercourse (cf. Gen 20:6 LXX). Paul neither endorses nor condemns the slogan — he immediately qualifies it with the concessive dia de tas porneias (but because of sexual immorality cases…).
2: dia de tas porneias (but because of sexual immorality cases) — the plural porneias implies recurring instances in the community. heautou gynaika/idion andra (his own wife / her own husband) — the reciprocal possession language is striking: each has their own spouse; neither spouse is communal property. This grammatical reciprocity anticipates the full mutual-authority language of v. 4.
3: tēn opheilomenēn eunonian apodidotō (let him fulfill the conjugal duty) — opheilomenēn (owed, due): marriage creates a debt structure; each spouse owes the other conjugal attention. Apodidōmi (give back, render) is the debt-payment verb — not gift language but obligation language. Paul frames marital sexual relations as a mutual duty, not a concession. This is among Paul's most egalitarian statements in a context where husbands routinely denied their wives access to their bodies.
4: hē gynē tou idiou sōmatos ouk exousiazei alla ho anēr; homoiōs de kai ho anēr — the mutual exousiazō (has authority over) is remarkable: each spouse has authority over the other's body, not merely their own. The statement is symmetrical (homoiōs de kai) — husband and wife are placed on identical terms regarding conjugal authority. In the context of Greco-Roman household ethics, where the wife was subordinate in all matters, this mutual claim on bodily access is counter-cultural.
5: mē apostereite allēlous (do not deprive each other) — apostereō (defraud, deprive) appears also at 6:8; here in the sexual context of mutual conjugal obligation. ei mē an ek symphōnou (except by mutual agreement) — the exception clause: abstinence is legitimate only when both agree. pros ton kairon (for a season) — temporary, bounded. scholasēte tē proseuchē (devote yourselves to prayer) — the purpose: ascetic abstinence for focused prayer, a recognized Jewish practice (cf. Joel 2:16; LXX 1 Sam 21:5). akrasia (lack of self-control) — Paul acknowledges desire as a real and potentially dangerous force.
6: touto de legō kata syngnōmēn, ou kat' epitagēn (I say this as a concession, not as a command) — Paul carefully distinguishes: what he says about the concession for abstinence (v. 5) is a syngnōmē (concession, permission), not a epitagē (divine command). This is an important hermeneutical marker: he is making a pastoral accommodation, not a theological absolute.
7: thelō de pantas anthrōpous einai hōs kai emauton (I wish that all people were like me) — Paul situates himself as a model of celibate life. The translation note flags the dispute: Paul's charisma (spiritual gift, v. 7b) for celibacy is a genuine gift (charisma is the standard Pauline term for Spirit-given capacity, cf. ch. 12), not a universal calling. hekastos idion charisma echei ek Theou, ho men houtōs, ho de houtōs (each has their own gift from God, one of one kind and another of another) — both marriage and celibacy are charismata; neither is superior in the sense that both are divine gifts.
8: tois agamois kai tais chērais (to the unmarried and to widows) — agamos (unmarried) is Paul's coinage for those who have never been married or who are no longer married; it appears only in this chapter. The recommendation meinai hōs kagō (to remain as I am) implies Paul himself is unmarried at this point — widowed (if previously married) or never married.
9: ei de ouk enkrateuontai, gamēsatōsan (but if they do not have self-control, let them marry) — enkrateuomai (have self-control, contain oneself) is the virtue of self-mastery prized in both Stoic ethics and Jewish piety. kreitton gar estin gamēsai ē pyrousthai (it is better to marry than to burn with passion) — pyrousthai (to burn, be inflamed): the metaphor of sexual desire as fire. Paul does not frame marriage as a necessary evil but as the appropriate remedy — kreitton (better), a comparative that implies both options have merit.
10: tois de gegamēkosi parangellō, ouk egō alla ho Kyrios (to the married I give this instruction — not from me but from the Lord) — Paul distinguishes sharply between his own judgment and a direct command from the Lord Jesus. Here he has an explicit saying of Jesus (cf. Mark 10:11-12; Matt 5:32): the wife (gynaika) must not separate (chōristhēnai) from her husband. Parangellō (command) is the authoritative instructional verb, stronger than legō (say) or gnōmēn didōmi (give my opinion, v. 25).
11: ean de kai chōristhē (but if she does separate) — Paul acknowledges that the separation may happen even against the command. His instruction in that case: remain unmarried (agamos menetō) or be reconciled (katallagētō) to her husband. Katallagē is Paul's reconciliation vocabulary (2 Cor 5:18-20; Rom 5:11) — here applied to marital breakdown. The possibility of reconciliation is kept open; permanent new remarriage is not authorized while the husband lives.
12: tois de loipois legō egō, ouch ho Kyrios (to the others I say — I, not the Lord) — Paul has no recorded saying of Jesus for this situation (mixed marriage). He explicitly flags this: his instruction carries apostolic authority but not a dominical command. Legō egō (I say, I) — the emphatic pronoun marks his own authority, distinct from the Lord's. The situation he addresses is post-conversion mixed marriage, not a believer marrying an unbeliever.
13: kai gynē ētis echei andra aiston (and a woman who has an unbelieving husband) — the symmetry: the situation applies equally to believing wife with unbelieving husband. mē aphietō ton andra (she should not divorce her husband) — the verb aphiēmi is the standard Greek divorce term. Paul's instruction is the same in both directions: do not initiate divorce from an unbelieving but consenting spouse.
14: hēgiastai gar ho anēr ho apistos en tē gynaiki (the unbelieving husband is made holy through his believing wife) — hēgiastai is perfect passive: a settled state of being-made-holy. This does not mean the unbelieving spouse is saved but that the household is brought within the sphere of sanctification by the presence of a believing partner. The translation note flags the dispute. Hagia (holy) applied to the children of such a union — the household participates in holiness by association.
15: ei de ho apistos chōrizetai, chōrizesthō (but if the unbelieving partner separates, let them go) — the exception: if the unbeliever initiates departure, the believer is not obligated to prevent it. ou dedoulōtai ho adelphos ē hē adelphē en tois toioutois (a brother or sister is not enslaved in such cases) — dedoulōtai (enslaved, bound) picks up the slavery metaphor that runs through ch. 7: marriage creates a binding, but the unbeliever's departure releases the binding. en eirēnē keklēken hēmas ho Theos (God has called us to peace) — the calling to peace overrides the compulsion to preserve a marriage that the unbeliever has already abandoned.
16: ti gar oidas, gynai, ei ton andra sōseis (for how do you know, wife, whether you will save your husband?) — the rhetorical question is a pastoral caution: do not stay in a destructive marriage on the grounds of the missionary hope, since that outcome is uncertain. The verse has been read optimistically (you might save them — so stay) or pessimistically (you might not save them — so don't chain yourself to that hope). The latter reading fits the context better: Paul is warning against treating the mixed marriage as a guaranteed mission field.
17: hōs ekklēsen ho Theos hekaston, houtōs peripatatō (let each person live as God has assigned them, as the Lord has called them) — the calling principle: the condition in which God's call found you is not a problem to be escaped but a vocation to be lived. Keklēken (has called) is the perfect: the call was made and remains in force. kai houtōs en tais ekklesiais pasais diatassō (this is my rule in all the churches) — Paul claims this as a universal apostolic teaching, not a local accommodation.
18: en peritomē eklēthē tis (was someone called while circumcised?) — the calling principle applied to Jewish identity. Epispasthō (let him not remove marks of circumcision) — epispasm: a surgical procedure used by some Jews to restore the appearance of foreskin, documented in 1 Macc 1:15. Paul forbids it: the marker of the prior covenant-identity need not be hidden. Similarly, the uncircumcised should not seek circumcision.
19: hē peritomē ouden estin kai hē akrobystia ouden estin, alla tērēsis entolōn Theou (circumcision is nothing and uncircumcision is nothing; keeping God's commands is what matters) — the radical relativization of the covenant marker: neither circumcision nor its absence has intrinsic value. What matters is entolōn tērēsis (keeping God's commands). This is not antinomian (Paul upholds commands) but anti-ethnic-marker: ethnic-covenant status does not constitute righteousness.
20: hekastos en tē klēsei hē eklēthē, en tautē menetō (let each person remain in the situation they were in when God called them) — the governing principle of vv. 17-24 stated plainly. Klēsis (calling, vocation): Paul may be using this in its sociological sense (condition: slave, free, circumcised, uncircumcised) or theological sense (divine call into Christ). Both are probably in view: God's call finds you in a particular condition and makes that condition the site of your vocation.
21: doulos eklēthēs; mē soi meletō (were you a slave when called? do not let it trouble you) — the advice is: your slave status does not impede your standing before God. all' ei kai dynasthai eleuthos genesthai, mallon chrēsai (but if you are indeed able to gain your freedom, take the opportunity) — the contested phrase. Mallon chrēsai (use/avail yourself more rather): grammatically ambiguous — take the freedom, or use your slave status (for the Lord). The majority of scholars now favor the manumission reading: if freedom is available, take it. Dynasthai (be able) implies an actual offer, not a hypothetical.
22: ho gar en Kyriō klētheis doulos apeleutheros Kyriou estin (the one who was a slave when called in the Lord is the Lord's freed person) — Paul applies the manumission metaphor to spiritual status: the slave who belongs to Christ has been set free (apeleutheros, freed person) in the most important sense. homoiōs ho eleutheros klētheis doulos Christou estin (similarly, the free person who was called is Christ's slave) — the free person, by coming to Christ, becomes a slave of Christ. The social asymmetry is inverted: freedom in Christ transcends and relativizes freedom in the world.
23: timēs ēgorasthēte; mē ginesthe douloi anthrōpōn (you were bought at a price; do not become slaves of people) — the same redemption-purchase formula as 6:20, now applied to social ethics: the purchased people of Christ must not trade their spiritual freedom for new social entanglements that compromise their primary allegiance. This is not a command to reject all social hierarchy but a warning against the patron-client dependencies that could compromise devotion to Christ.
24: hekastos en hō eklēthē, adelphoi, en toutō menetō para Theō (brothers and sisters, each of you should remain before God in the situation you were in when called) — the principle restated a third time, now with the addition para Theō (before God / with God). The presence of God reframes the social location: whatever your condition, you are standing before God in it. Translation note flags a dispute on para Theō: with God (in relationship) or before God (in God's sight).
25: peri de tōn parthenōn epitagēn Kyriou ouk echō, gnōmēn de didōmi (now about those who have never been married: I have no command from the Lord, but I give my judgment) — Paul is transparent about his epistemic status. Parthenōn (virgins, those who have never married) is the subject; he has no dominical tradition for this case. Gnōmēn didōmi (I give my judgment/opinion) — a weaker speech-act than parangellō (command) or legō without qualification. Pistos einai hypo Kyriou (to be trustworthy by the Lord's mercy) — his authority is mercy-given, not self-generated.
26: enestōsan anankēn (the present crisis) — anankē (necessity, crisis, compulsion): scholars debate what this refers to — persecution, famine, the general eschatological urgency of the age, or a local Corinthian situation. The word elsewhere in Paul (Rom 8:35; 2 Cor 12:10) describes tribulation. Enestōsan (present, standing near): the crisis is already upon them. In view of this urgency, remaining in one's current condition is tactically wise.
27: dedesai gynaiki; mē zētei lysin (are you committed to a wife? do not seek release) — dedesai (have been bound) is the perfect passive of the same verb used for legal binding. The symmetry of the verse is complete: the bound should not seek release; the free should not seek binding. Both options have equal standing; neither is commanded.
28: thlipsin de tē sarki hexousin hoi toioutoi (those who marry will face practical hardships) — thlipsis (tribulation, pressure): Paul is not saying marriage is sinful but that it introduces real-world complications in a time of eschatological pressure. Tē sarki (in the flesh): the hardships are material and embodied, not merely spiritual. Translation note flags sarx here as neutral (embodied life), not the ethical sense.
29: ho kairos synestalmenos estin (the time is contracted/compressed) — synestalmenos is a nautical-textile metaphor: a sail gathered in, a garment rolled up. Not merely short but pulled tight, urgent, wound up. The phrase anchors the whole of vv. 29-31: the compressed urgency of the eschatological moment means existing social structures should be worn lightly. Those with wives should live hōs mē echontes (as though not having them) — not abandoning them but not being absorbed by the relationship.
30: The fourfold hōs mē (as though not) series — mourning/not mourning, rejoicing/not rejoicing, buying/not owning — is Paul's eschatological detachment ethic. The present-age structures (grief, joy, commerce) are real but temporary. Hōs mē does not counsel withdrawal from these realities but a held-loosely engagement that refuses ultimate investment in them.
31: to schēma tou kosmou toutou paragetai (the present form of this world is passing away) — schēma (form, fashion, outward shape): not the world itself but its present configuration. Paragetai (is passing away) — present tense: the transition is already underway, not merely anticipated. Paul's eschatology is inaugurated: the age to come has begun its incursion into the present age through Christ's resurrection.
32: ho agamos merimnā ta tou Kyriou, pōs areskē tō Kyriō (the unmarried man is concerned about the things of the Lord — how to please the Lord) — merimnā (is anxious/concerned for): in Phil 4:6 Paul forbids anxiety; here he commends it, when it is directed toward the Lord. The point is undivided attention. Areskō (please): the goal of the celibate life is pleasing the Lord; the goal of the married life is also pleasing, but now divided between Lord and spouse.
33: ho de gamēsas merimnā ta tou kosmou, pōs areskē tē gynaiki (the married man is concerned about worldly affairs — how to please his wife) — Paul does not condemn this; pleasing one's spouse is a legitimate and good concern. But the structural division (memeristai, v. 34: is divided) is real. The celibate is not morally superior but has a structural simplicity in orientation.
34: memeristai (is divided) — perfect passive: a state of ongoing dividedness. hē agamos kai hē parthenos merimnā ta tou Kyriou (the unmarried woman or virgin is concerned about the Lord's things) — the symmetry with the male situation (vv. 32-33) is maintained: the unmarried woman has the same structural advantage as the unmarried man. Hagia kai tō sōmati kai tō pneumati (holy in body and spirit) — the celibate life is a whole-person holiness, not merely spiritual.
35: pros to hymōn sympheron legō (I say this for your own benefit) — Paul frames his preference for celibacy as pastoral care, not imposition. ouch hina brochos hymin epibalō (not to restrict you, literally not to throw a noose on you) — the metaphor of a restraint or snare: Paul denies that he is trying to limit freedom. pros to euschēmon kai euparedron tō Kyriō aperspastōs (to promote what is fitting and to enable undivided devotion to the Lord) — euparedron (sitting well beside, devoted attendance): a rare term meaning devoted proximity, continuous attendance. Undivided devotion (aperispastōs) is the goal.
36: ei de tis aschēmonein epi tēn parthenon autou nomizei (but if anyone thinks he is acting dishonorably toward his betrothed virgin) — the situation: a man betrothed to a virgin, constrained by the general principle of v. 26 (remain as you are), but now under social pressure that his restraint dishonors her. Hyperakmos (past her prime, beyond the peak of marriageable age): implies the woman is aging without marriage. Paul's pastoral response: if the man feels this obligation, let them marry — he does not sin.
37: hos de hestēken en tē kardia autou hedraios (but the man who stands firm in his commitment) — hedraios (settled, immovable, like a foundation): the architectural metaphor of stability. Three conditions: firm in commitment (hedraios), under no compulsion (mē echōn anankēn), desire under his own authority (exousian peri tou idiou thelēmatos). The man who freely chooses to keep his betrothed as his betrothed does well — the choice is genuinely free and honored.
38: hōste kai ho gamizōn tēn heautou parthenon kalōs poiei, kai ho mē gamizōn kreitton poiēsei (so the one who marries his betrothed does well, and the one who refrains from marrying does even better) — both options are kalōs (well); only the celibate option is kreitton (better). The superlative is comparative, not absolute: both are good; one has a contextual advantage. Paul will not forbid marriage; he will not pretend the advantage does not exist.
39: gynē dedetal hō chronō ean zē ho anēr autēs (a wife is bound to her husband as long as he lives) — dedetal (is bound): the same binding-verb as v. 27. Death dissolves the marital bond. Translation note flags nomos: the law by which she is bound may be Torah-law or the general principle. monon en Kyriō (only in the Lord) — the restriction on remarriage after the husband's death: the new marriage must be within the community of faith.
40: makariotēra de estin ean houtōs meinē (she will be happier if she remains as she is) — makariotēra: comparative of makarios (blessed), the beatitude-word. Paul's final opinion: the widow who remains unmarried is more blessed. dokō de kagō pneuma Theou echein (and I think I also have the Spirit of God) — a dry, careful claim: dokō (I think, I believe): not asserting certainty but claiming the same Spirit-guidance he has appealed to throughout. The modest self-defense implies someone in Corinth disputed whether Paul spoke with spiritual authority on these matters.
